Summary: ApoE4 is the primary genetic risk factor for sporadic Alzheimer’s disease. ApoE4 translocates to the nucleus and regulates expression of genes involved in aging, Aβ production, inflammation, and apoptosis. ApoE4 interacts with mitochondria and causes mitochondrial dysfunction and neurotoxicity that are associated with increased mitochondrial Ca2+ and reactive oxygen species levels. ApoE4 alters mitochondrial dynamics, including fusion, fission, and mitophagy. Mitochondrial dysfunction associated with ApoE4 is related to neurodegeneration. ApoE4 is linked to Aβ aggregation and clearance and to tau-related neurofibrillary tangles. Emerging hypotheses have linked ApoE4 to disturbed synaptic plasticity, altered glial responses and neuroinflammation, blood–brain barrier disruption, astrocyte-associated lipid metabolism, and neurotoxic ApoE4 fragments. Because mitochondrial alterations are an early feature of Alzheimer’s disease and ApoE4 is the major genetic risk factor, studying ApoE4 mechanisms may be crucial to identify therapeutic targets.
Reference: Pires, M., & Rego, A. C. (2023). Apoe4 and Alzheimer's Disease Pathogenesis-Mitochondrial Deregulation and Targeted Therapeutic Strategies. Int J Mol Sci, 24(1), 778. https://doi.org/10.3390/ijms24010778

Summary: APOE4 is the single greatest genetic risk factor for sporadic Alzheimer’s disease. Researchers studied APOE4 in CRISPR/Cas9-edited human iPSC-derived brain cells and in isogenic human iPSC-derived neurons, astrocytes, and microglia-like cells. Transcriptional profiling found cell-type-specific expression changes affecting synaptic function in neurons, lipid metabolism in astrocytes, and immune response in microglia-like cells. In neurons, APOE4 was associated with increased synapse number and elevated Aβ42 secretion versus isogenic APOE3. In astrocytes, APOE4 was associated with impaired Aβ uptake and cholesterol accumulation. In microglia-like cells, APOE4 was associated with altered morphologies that correlated with reduced Aβ phagocytosis. Conversion of APOE4 to APOE3 in sporadic AD patient-derived iPSCs alleviated most AD-related phenotypes in neurons, glia, and organoids. However, Aβ42 secretion was not reduced in APOE3 versus APOE4 sporadic AD neurons. Reduction in early endosome numbers in APOE3 sporadic AD neurons was not statistically significant despite a trend versus APOE4. The authors reported that additional factors in that sporadic AD iPSC-derived cell line may confound the observed AD-related pathologies.
Reference: Lin, Y. T., Seo, J., Gao, F., Feldman, H. M., Wen, H. L., Penney, J., Cam, H. P., Gjoneska, E., Raja, W. K., Cheng, J., Rueda, R., Kritskiy, O., Abdurrob, F., Peng, Z., Milo, B., Yu, C. J., Elmsaouri, S., Dey, D., Ko, T., Yankner, B. A., & Tsai, L. H. (2018). APOE4 Causes Widespread Molecular and Cellular Alterations Associated with Alzheimer's Disease Phenotypes in Human iPSC-Derived Brain Cell Types. Neuron, 98(6), 1141-1154.e7. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neuron.2018.05.008

Summary: In AD model mice bearing the three common human APOE alleles, APOE4 is associated with an increased age-dependent frequency of terminally inflammatory microglia (TIMs). TIMs concomitantly expressed inflammatory signals and cell-intrinsic stress markers. In APOE4 mice, TIM transcriptional subpopulation composition shifted toward stress-marker–high, effector-lo states. APOE2 mice showed predominantly effector-hi TIMs, and APOE3 mice were not enriched for effector-hi TIMs relative to APOE4. TIMs exhibited defects in amyloid-β clearance and altered cell–cell communication during aducanumab treatment. An analogous TIM population was detected in cortical tissue of human AD patients. TIMs may represent an exhausted-like inflammatory microglial state that contributes to AD risk and pathology in APOE4 carriers and the elderly. TIMs thus present a potential therapeutic target.
Reference: Millet, A., Ledo, J. H., & Tavazoie, S. F. (2024). An exhausted-like microglial population accumulates in aged and APOE4 genotype Alzheimer's brains. Immunity, 57(1), 153-170.e6. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.immuni.2023.12.001

Summary: ApoE4 is the strongest genetic risk factor for late-onset Alzheimer’s disease and an AD risk allele in the AD brain. ApoE4 is not required to induce AD but it can accelerate disease progression. ApoE4 could initiate and accelerate Aβ accumulation and aggregation in the brain, and it influences Aβ deposition. ApoE4 could increase the level of Aβ oligomers in the brain and influence Aβ clearance. ApoE4 affects tau phosphorylation and could affect the density of neurofibrillary tangles (NFTs) in the brain. ApoE4 hydrolytic fragments are linked to tau pathology. Compared with ApoE3 and ApoE2, ApoE4 is less efficient at supplying cholesterol and at maintaining synaptic integrity and plasticity. ApoE4 exacerbates oxidative stress. ApoE4 is suspected to associate with mitochondrial dysfunction, neuroinflammation, and sleep disturbances, and it affects cerebrovascular integrity in AD brains. Therapeutic targeting of ApoE4 can slow AD progression and reduce disease occurrence in ApoE4 populations.
Reference: Sun, Y. Y., Wang, Z., & Huang, H. C. (2023). Roles of ApoE4 on the Pathogenesis in Alzheimer's Disease and the Potential Therapeutic Approaches. Cell Mol Neurobiol, 43(7), 3115-3136. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10571-023-01365-1

Summary:
Systematic: role → mechanism → outcome.

APOE4 is a major shared risk factor for several neurodegenerative diseases and is the strongest genetic risk factor for late-onset Alzheimer’s disease. This prominence is partly attributed to APOE4’s role in lipid metabolism and related inflammation and to its modulation of brain innate immune responses. APOE4 is implicated at the interface of inflammation and neurodegeneration via glial-mediated mechanisms in the CNS. Peripheral and CNS ApoE pools are reported to function independently. In mice, restoring peripheral ApoE in animals lacking brain ApoE rescues learning and memory deficits, implying peripheral ApoE can influence the CNS via the vasculature. In humans, APOE4 carriers show higher IL-8 and TNFα after cardiopulmonary bypass, and chronic low-grade peripheral inflammation in carriers links to increased AD risk and earlier onset. Immune-challenged APOE4 mice given LPS produce higher TNFα, IL-6 and nitric oxide. APOE4 carriers show earlier amyloid-β deposition and clinical onset, faster disease progression, heavier Aβ plaque burden, and increased brain atrophy compared to non‑APOE4 carriers. In Alzheimer’s disease, APOE4 is associated with increased tau pathology, particularly when amyloid pathology is present. APOE4-positive cases have greater baseline Aβ and tau burden and a higher tau accumulation rate, suggesting an Aβ-independent effect of APOE4 on vulnerability to progressive tau accumulation in AD. APOE4-associated tau pathology is increased in the medial temporal lobe where APOE mRNA expression is highest, and these findings point to tau pathology as a possible culprit for medial temporal neurodegeneration in APOE4 carriers. APOE4 is also linked to increased CSF tau levels in AD, particularly in women.
Reference: Parhizkar, S., & Holtzman, D. M. (2022). APOE mediated neuroinflammation and neurodegeneration in Alzheimer's disease. Semin Immunol, 59, 101594. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.smim.2022.101594
